title: practical english usage - Part 244
content:
For details of other kinds of tags. ►    299.

You wants them bricks today, innit?

In non-standard speech, isn't it? / innit? is common as a universal question tag.

This is your last chance, right? I'm not working extra hours, right?

Right can also be used, rather aggressively, to mean ‘ Is that clear to you?' You haven't got a ticket, right?

We're seeing her tomorrow, no? They all speak English, right?

very often used to check  information or ask for agreement.

In informal speech, simpler tags such as No? (after affirmatives) and Right? are 10 simpler      question       tags I suppose you're hungry, aren't you?

I don't think it will rain, will it? (     ... do I?) I think he's Norwegian, isn't he? (Nor... don't I?) similar expressions (►  277).

Note the use of question tags in sentences beginning with I (don't) think and 9 I (don't)    think Jack be here tomorrow, will he?

Have a good time, did you? Your mother at home, is she?

with ellipsis.

In very informal speech, a question tag can sometimes be used after a question Speech and Spoken Exchanges   Section 27 grammar •  308   short answers: Yes, he can, etc In American English, nor is not normally used after and.

not                                                h Ruth didn't turn up, and neither/nor did Kate, (     .. . and Kate-didn't (< >.) not 7 can't swim.' 'Neither/nor can I.' (     I also can't.) auxiliary verb + subject.

at the beginning of a clause, and are followed by inverted word order (►   270): We   can use neither and nor as adverbs to mean ‘ also not’ . Neither and nor come 2  neither        do   /, / can't   either,       etc not 'I'm getting bored.' 'Me too.' (     Fal$&) A  more informal alternative to So am I, etc is Me too.

We   can say, for example, So can her sister, but not So can hcr-sister dance.

We   do not normally use a more complete verb phrase in this structure.

'He just wants the best for his country.' 'So did Hitler.' After a clause with no auxiliary verb, we  use do!does!did.

7 have a headache.' 'So have I.' I was tired, and so were the others.

The  same structure is possible with non-auxiliary be and have.

'I've lost their address.' 'So have I.' Louise can dance beautifully, and so can her sister.

The  structure is used to answer or add to what came before. Note the word order.

We   can use so to mean 'similarly, also', before auxiliary verb + subject.

1  so   + auxiliary       + subject 309 so   am    /, neither        do   they,     etc For similar structures, ►    307  (reply questions), ►    305-306  (question tags) and ►    279 (ellipsis).

Yes, I am. (     Yes,-I'm.) Note that stressed, non-contracted affirmative forms are used in short answers.

'Nice day.' 'Yes, it is, isn't it?

Short answers can be followed by tags (►  305-306).

'Have they any money? 'Yes, they have.' *Is she happy? '1 think she is.' Non-auxiliary be and have can be used in short answers.

'That surprised you.' 'It certainly did.' 'She likes cakes.' 'She really does.' We use do if there is no other auxiliary.

'You didn't phone Debbie last night.' 'No, but I did this morning.' 'Don't forget to write.' 7 won't.' 'Are you enjoying yourself? '1 certainly am.' 'Has it stopped raining? 'No, it hasn't.' 'Can he swim? 'Yes, he can.' (More natural than Yes, he can swim.) auxiliary verb, together with whatever other words are really necessary.

words that have just been said. A  common 'short answer' pattern is subject + Answers are often grammatically incomplete, because they do not need to repeat 308 short    answers:     Yes,    he   can,    etc grammar •  310   politeness: using questions Carry this for me, please.      You had better help me.

Help me, would you?        You should shut the door.

Please help me for a few minutes.  You ought to tell me your plans.

or advice, but they are not polite ways of requesting people to do things.

The  following structures can be used perfectly correctly to give orders, instructions order or instruction a little more polite, but does not turn it into a request.

in conversation with strangers or people we  do not know well. Please makes an These structures can therefore seem rude if we  use them in requests, especially not asking people to do things, but telling or advising them to do things.
